coredata.dc:description: Efficient food packaging is essential for maintaining quality and extending the shelf life of food products. This study proposes an innovative and eco-friendly approach to developing multifunctional packaging films enhanced with carbon dots (CDs). Derived from Sargassum fusiforme polysaccharide (SFP) extraction residues, CDs synthesized using waste ethanol (E-CDs) showed more uniform morphology and enriched carbonyl (C=O) and imine (N-C=N) functional groups compared to those produced in water (W-CDs). Multifunctional composite films were produced by incorporating these CDs into a gelatin/SFP matrix, achieving notable improvements in mechanical strength, UV resistance, thermal stability, and hydrophobicity, with optimal results at 1.0% E-CDs concentration. The gelatin/SFP/E-CDs-1.0% film displayed exceptional antioxidant activity and antibacterial efficacy against S. aureus and E. coli. Practical applications demonstrated that this material effectively extended the shelf life and maintained the quality of blueberries. This research underscores the potential of circular economy practices by upcycling marine seaweed residues into high-performance, eco-friendly food packaging, presenting a sustainable solution to current challenges in the field.

head.ce:title: Designing high-performance fruit packaging: Gelatin films enriched with marine seaweed polysaccharides and carbon dots

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Efficient food packaging is essential for maintaining quality and extending the shelf life of food products. This study proposes an innovative and eco-friendly approach to developing multifunctional packaging films enhanced with carbon dots (CDs). Derived from Sargassum fusiforme polysaccharide (SFP) extraction residues, CDs synthesized using waste ethanol (E-CDs) showed more uniform morphology and enriched carbonyl (C=O) and imine (N-C=N) functional groups compared to those produced in water (W-CDs). Multifunctional composite films were produced by incorporating these CDs into a gelatin/SFP matrix, achieving notable improvements in mechanical strength, UV resistance, thermal stability, and hydrophobicity, with optimal results at 1.0% E-CDs concentration. The gelatin/SFP/E-CDs-1.0% film displayed exceptional antioxidant activity and antibacterial efficacy against S. aureus and E. coli. Practical applications demonstrated that this material effectively extended the shelf life and maintained the quality of blueberries. This research underscores the potential of circular economy practices by upcycling marine seaweed residues into high-performance, eco-friendly food packaging, presenting a sustainable solution to current challenges in the field.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Food packaging is essential for maintaining food quality, ensuring safety, and extending shelf life by protecting food from environmental and microbial spoilage (Zhu et al., 2025). Effective packaging prevents texture degradation, preserves flavor, and minimizes food waste, making it a cornerstone of modern food systems (Ribeiro et al., 2024). However, the global reliance on conventional plastic packaging materials, derived primarily from petrochemicals, poses severe environmental challenges. These materials are not only non-biodegradable but also contribute significantly to land and ocean pollution, raising urgent concerns about their sustainability (Marsh & Bugusu, 2007; Shaikh, Yaqoob, & Aggarwa, 2021).

Biodegradable polymers such as gelatin (Guo et al., 2024), cellulose (Shankar & Rhim, 2016), and starch (Ribeiro et al., 2024) have emerged as promising alternatives due to their renewable and eco-friendly nature. Despite their potential, these materials often lack critical functional properties such as mechanical strength, water resistance, and thermal stability, limiting their practical application in food packaging (Zhang & Youngblood, 2023). Zhang, Zhao, and Shi (2016) noted that gelatin-based films exhibit poor thermal and UV irradiation stability, leading to quality degradation during food storage. Moreover, the growing demand for active packaging has introduced new challenges in food packaging development. These materials, which offer antioxidant and antimicrobial properties, often rely on functional additives that are synthetic, costly, or environmentally unfriendly (Bang, Roy, & Rhim, 2022).

Carbon dots (CDs), quasi-spherical zero-dimensional nanomaterials with sizes under 10 nm, offer significant potential for enhancing the performance of food packaging films due to their unique structure, photostability, ease of synthesis, and biocompatibility (Chen et al., 2023a; Li et al., 2024; Lv et al., 2024). The presence of surface defects and unpaired electrons imparts free radical scavenging capacities to CDs, while their photoelectric and photothermal properties also contribute to antibacterial applications (Chen et al., 2023b; Dong et al., 2023). Sattariazar, Arsalani, and Ebrahimi (2023) highlighted the excellent antioxidant and UV absorption properties of banana peel-derived CDs, attributing these to the abundant n-π conjugated groups on the nanomaterial surface. Additionally, the low surface energy of CDs facilitates their uniform dispersion in hydrogels, improving the mechanical strength and structural integrity of the gelatin-based films (Cui, Ren, Wang, & Sun, 2020). CDs can also function as interfacial agents, modifying interactions between different polymers, and affecting the transmission rate to specific gases (Sattariazar et al., 2023). Consequently, incorporating CDs into a gelatin-based hydrogel matrix holds promise for enhancing the functional performance of packaging films. Unfortunately, research on the integration of CDs into food packaging films remains in its early stages.

While the hydrothermal method has been widely adopted for synthesizing CDs, most studies have focused on utilizing forest and agricultural biowastes, such as sawdust (Momo, Doumbi, Waffo, Noumi, & Tchatchueng, 2024) and banana peel (Sattariazar et al., 2023). Macroalgae biowastes represent a largely untapped resource for CDs synthesis. The extraction of polysaccharides from macroalgae typically involves water as the extraction medium, followed by ethanol precipitation to isolate the polysaccharide (Liu & Xin, 2023). This process generates significant wastes, including post-extraction residues and waste ethanol, a mixture containing approximately 80% ethanol and 20% water by volume. Direct disposal of these wastes not only results in resource inefficiency but also poses environmental concerns. From a circular economy perspective, repurposing these by-products into value-added products, such as CDs, offers a sustainable pathway to minimize waste and optimize resource utilization.

This study aims to address these challenges by utilizing polysaccharide extraction residues from macroalgae as precursors for synthesizing CDs through the hydrothermal method. Waste ethanol was chosen as the synthesis medium, and the resulting CDs were evaluated for key properties such as morphology, particle size, functional groups, and optical characteristics. The synthesized CDs were then incorporated into a gelatin-based hydrogel matrix to produce multifunctional composite films. These films were systematically evaluated for their microstructure, hydrophobicity, thermal stability, water resistance, UV-shielding ability, antioxidant activity, and antibacterial efficacy. Additionally, the films' effectiveness in preserving blueberries was evaluated using key indicators, including decay incidence, darkening incidence, commercially acceptable rate, weight loss, soluble solids content, and anthocyanin content. A schematic overview of this work is presented in Fig. 1 . By integrating CDs synthesized from marine biowastes into active food packaging films, this work contributes to addressing critical challenges in food preservation and sustainability. The findings underscore the potential of circular economy practices in utilizing marine biomass waste to develop high-performance, eco-friendly packaging materials.

Sargassum fusiforme (SF) was procured from Huiyi Food Co., Ltd, ground, and passed through a 100-mesh sieve. Sargassum fusiforme polysaccharides (SFP) were extracted following the method outlined in our previous study (Luan et al., 2024). Gelatin was procured from Solarbio Co., Ltd (CAS: 9000-70-8). Ethanol, glycerol, sodium chloride, reduced iron, potassium persulfate, and hydrochloric acid were obtained from Chengdu Kelon Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Chengdu, China). Activated carbon was purchased from Yuanyang Co., Ltd (Chengde, China). Luria-Bertani (LB) medium was sourced from Qingdao Hope BioTechnology Co., Ltd. (Qingdao, China). Escherichia coli and Staphylococcus aureus used in this study were obtained from the American Type Culture Collection (ATCC, Manassas, VA, USA). Fresh blueberries (Vaccinium corymbosum L. ‘Eureka’) with uniform size were purchased from a local orchard of Binyuan Biotechnology Co., Ltd (Fuzhou, China).

CDs were synthesized using a hydrothermal method with slight modifications from a previous study (Cui et al., 2023). Briefly, 1 g of SF residue was dissolved in either distilled water or waste ethanol within a polytetrafluoroethylene (Teflon)-lined reactor. The reaction was carried out at either 175 °C or 200 °C for 6 h. After the reaction, the mixture was centrifuged at 12,000 rpm for 10 min. The supernatant was then filtered through a 0.22 μm pore-sized filter. This process generated four types of CDs: E-CDs-175 °C, E-CDs-200 °C (synthesized using waste ethanol), and W-CDs-175 °C, W-CDs-200 °C (synthesized using water). The supernatant was stored at 4 °C for subsequent analysis.

CDs morphology was analyzed by high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (TEM, FEI Talos F200S, Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) at an accelerated voltage of 200 kV. Particle size distribution (PSD) was determined by analyzing 100 points from the corresponding TEM images. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were measured using an X'Pert3 Powder diffractometer (PANalytical B.V., Almelo, Netherlands) at a scan rate of 0.5°/min over a 2θ range of 10–80°, with a tube pressure of 40 kV and current of 40 mA. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were recorded with an Escalab 250Xi system (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Waltham, USA). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were acquired using a Nicolet 6700 spectrometer (Thermo Electron Co., Ltd Waltham, MA, USA) over a range of 500–4000 cm−1 with 32 scans. Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were recorded using a Cary60 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Agilent, Santa Clara, USA). Fluorescence measurements were performed using an RF-6700 Fluorescence spectrometer (Shimadzu, Tokyo, Japan) with a data interval of 1 nm and a scan rate of 6000 nm/min.

Composite films were fabricated using the solution casting method after adjusting the CDs to various concentrations (0.1%, 0.5%, and 1.0%) (Gong et al., 2024; Guo et al., 2024). A 100 mL solution of CDs was mixed with 2.0 g of gelatin, 1.0 g of SFP, and 0.9 g of glycerol. The mixture underwent heat-induced cross-linking at 50 °C for 2 h. Afterward, the film samples were casted and dried at 45 °C for 10 h. The resulting films doped with 0.1%, 0.5%, and 1.0% E-CDs-175 °C were designated as gelatin/SFP/E-CDs-0.1%, gelatin/SFP/E-CDs-0.5%, and gelatin/SFP/E-CDs-1.0%, respectively. Similarly, films doped with 0.1%, 0.5%, and 1.0% W-CDs-200 °C were labeled as gelatin/SFP/W-CDs-0.1%, gelatin/SFP/W-CDs-0.5%, and gelatin/SFP/W-CDs-1.0%, respectively. For control samples, a pure gelatin film was prepared by mixing 2.0 g of gelatin with 100 mL of distilled water. A binary gelatin/SFP film was developed by mixing 2.0 g of gelatin, 1.0 g of SFP, and 0.9 g of glycerol under the same experimental conditions. These control films were referred to as gelatin and gelatin/SFP, respectively. All films were stored in a 25 °C, 50% relative humidity (RH) incubator for subsequent testing.

The rheological properties of the gelatin-based film solutions were evaluated using an AR 1500 Rheometer (TA Instruments, UK) with a plate-plate geometry (cone diameter 40 mm, gap 0.100 mm, angle 2°), following the method of Wei and Gao (2016) with slight modifications. Flow curves were recorded through steady-state flow measurements over a shear rate range of 0.1–100 s−1, and rheological parameters such as apparent viscosity and shear stress were obtained.

The mechanical properties of the composite films, including tensile strength (TS), elongation at break (EB), and toughness, were measured according to a previously reported method (Guo et al., 2024) with minor modifications. Each film was cut to a size of 50 mm × 25 mm and tested using an electronic universal testing machine (UTM-4204, Sunstest Technology Co., Ltd., Shenzhen, China) at a speed of 25 mm/min.

The film samples were analyzed using a Nicolet iS5 spectrophotometer (MA, USA), with spectra measured in the wavenumber range of 4000 to 400 cm−1 at a resolution of 4 cm−1 (Zhao, Zhang, Mujumdar, Adhikari, & Wang, 2022). Thermal properties of the composite films were measured using a Mettler Toledo Star TGA/DSC1 unit (Mettler Toledo Corp., Zurich, Switzerland). Approximately 5 mg of each film sample was heated from 30 °C to 600 °C at a rate of 10 °C/min under a nitrogen atmosphere (Purkayastha et al., 2014).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Light-shielding ability and water contact angle (WCA)
ce:section.ce:para: The light-shielding ability of the films was evaluated as described in a previous report (Guo et al., 2024). Each composite film was cut into pieces of 2 cm × 1 cm, and their light absorption at wavelengths ranging from 200 nm to 800 nm was recorded using a UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV-2450, Shimadzu Corporation, Japan). The WCA of the films' surface was measured following the method described by Li et al. (2023) using a contact angle analyzer (OCA50, Dataphysics, Germany). Each film was placed on a horizontally movable sample table, and a 4 μL drop of distilled water was applied to the films' surface from a syringe. The WCA on both sides of the drop was measured immediately.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Oxygen transmission rate (OTR) and water vapor permeability (WVP)
ce:section.ce:para:
The OTR of different film samples was assessed according to a previously reported method (Mu et al., 2023) with slight modifications. A mixture consisting of 1.5 g of sodium chloride, 0.5 g of reduced iron, and 2.0 g of activated carbon was placed into a weighing bottle (40 mm × 40 mm). The film was then placed over the mouth of the bottle and secured with a rubber band. The setup was stored in a container at 90% RH and 25 °C. The OTR was calculated using the following equation:
(1)
OTR = ΔM/(S × t)
where ΔM is the mass change of the mixture, S is the acreage of film, and t is the equilibration time.

The WVP of the composite films was determined according to the method described by Wang, Cen, Chen, and Fu (2020). Each film sample was sealed to a weighing bottle (40 mm × 40 mm) containing water. The bottle was then placed in an environmental chamber maintained at 25 °C and 75% RH. The bottles were weighed periodically until they reached a steady state. The water vapor transmission rate (WVTR) of the film was calculated as the slope obtained from the regression analysis of the moisture weight gain (Δw) transferred through the film area (S) over the equilibration time (t). The WVP can be expressed using the following equations:
(2)
WVTR = Δw / (S × t)
(3)
WVP = WVTR × (L / Δp)
where L is the thickness of the film, and Δp is the water vapor pressure difference between the two sides of the film.

The 2, 2-Diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl (DPPH) radical-scavenging activity was determined based on the method of Blois (1958). A 0.1 mM DPPH solution was prepared by dissolving DPPH in 95% ethanol. 15 μL of each film solution was added to 200 μL of the DPPH-ethanol solution, with ethanol alone serving as the blank. The mixture was incubated at 37 °C for 30 min, after which the absorbance at 517 nm was measured using a microplate reader (PerkinElmer, EnSPIre 2300 multimode). The DPPH-scavenging rate was then calculated as follows:
(4)
DPPH scavenging rate (%) = [(A
blank − A
sample )/A
blank ] × 100

The 2,2′-Azino-bis-3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonic acid (ABTS) radical-scavenging activity was determined according to the method of Re et al. (1999). An ABTS solution was prepared by mixing 7 mM ABTS with 2.5 mM potassium persulfate. The mixture was incubated at 37 °C in the dark for 16 h, then diluted 50-fold with PBS (phosphate buffer saline). The ABTS-scavenging capacity was assessed by adding 15 μL of each film-forming solution or PBS (blank) to 200 μL of the ABTS radical solution. Absorbance at 734 nm was measured using a microplate reader (PerkinElmer, EnSPIre 2300 multimode). The ABTS-scavenging rate was calculated as follows:
(5)
ABTS scavenging rate (%) = [(A
blank − A
sample )/A
blank ] × 100

The antibacterial activity of the film samples against Gram-negative E. coli and Gram-positive S. aureus was evaluated according to a method with some modifications (Guo et al., 2024). The tested strains were cultured in Luria-Bertani (LB) liquid medium in a thermostatic shaker at 37 °C for 24 h. The bacterial suspension was then diluted with LB liquid medium to a concentration of approximately 1.0 × 106 CFU/mL, and 100 μL was added to a 96-well plate, mixed with 50 μL of film solution. In the control group, 100 μL of the bacterial suspension was combined with 50 μL of distilled water. The mixture was subjected to UV irradiation (320 nm, 7.22 mW cm−2) for 5 min or left unirradiated to determine whether the film solution exhibited enhanced antibacterial activity due to the potential photosensitizing role of CDs. Under UV exposure, CDs could generate reactive oxygen species (ROS) through a photodynamic mechanism, contributing to bacterial eradication. After further incubation at 37 °C for 24 h, the colonies were photographed, and the antibacterial activity was quantified by measuring the absorbance at 600 nm. The bacterial survival rate was determined as follows:
(6)
Antibacterial rate (%) = (1-(A
t – A
c )/A
c ) × 100
where A
t is the absorbance value of treatment group, and A
c is the absorbance value of the control group.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of composite film in blueberry preservation
ce:section.ce:para: Blueberries, selected for uniform size, shape, and ripeness, were sourced from local orchards (Fuzhou Binyuan Biotechnology Co., Ltd) with no mechanical damage or pest symptoms. They were randomly divided into five groups: control (CK), gelatin, gelatin/SFP, gelatin/SFP/E-CDs, and gelatin/SFP/W-CDs. Blueberries from each treatment group, except for the control, were immersed in the respective film solutions for 1 min, followed by air-drying at 25 °C and 50–55% RH for 60 min. All treated and control fruit were subsequently stored under identical conditions (25 °C, 50–55% RH). During ambient storage, samples were collected every 2 days for analysis. The blueberry quality indicators, including decay incidence, darkening incidence, weight loss rate, commercially acceptable rate, firmness, total soluble solids content, malondialdehyde content, total anthocyanin content, were assessed based on the method provided in the Supplementary Materials.

All experimental procedures were conducted in triplicate. The results were presented as mean values ± standard deviations. Statistical differences between samples were assessed using a one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA), followed by Tukey's multiple comparisons test, conducted with Minitab Statistical Software. A p-value less than 0.05 denotes statistical significance.

SF residue was utilized to synthesize CDs using either waste ethanol or distilled water at temperatures (175 °C and 200 °C). TEM analysis revealed distinct morphological characteristics of the resulting CDs (Fig. 2 ). CDs synthesized with distilled water at 175 °C had an aggregated and irregular morphology with an average size of 18.2 nm. However, increasing the temperature to 200 °C produced well-dispersed CDs with an average size of 3.71 nm and a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, aligning with the findings of Yin et al. (2023). In contrast, the use of waste ethanol as the solvent led to evenly dispersed CDs, with no significant difference in particle size distribution or morphology between E-CDs-175 °C and E-CDs-200 °C. This indicated that 175 °C was sufficient for CDs synthesis while using waste ethanol. It was worth mentioning that the particle size of these CDs was markedly smaller than those products derived from Sophora Japonica and Anthrarufin (Guo et al., 2024; Mandal, Prasad, Mandal, & Das, 2019), which confirmed that SFP was an ideal material for development of CDs.

The FTIR analysis (Fig. 3b) further investigated the structural characteristics of the CDs. All samples exhibited a broad peak around 3350 cm−1, indicating the presence of -OH and/or -NH groups. The peak at 1734 cm−1, associated with C=O, was more pronounced in E-CDs than in W-CDs. Additional peaks at 658, 1108, 1231, 1367, 1430, 1475, and 1613 cm−1 corresponded to the presence of olefinic C−H (out-of-plane), C−O or C−O−C, carboxylate groups, C−NH2, -CH2, C−N, and C=C/C=N, respectively (Olmos-Moya, Velazquez-Martinez, Pineda-Arellano, Rangel-Mendez, & Chazaro-Ruiz, 2022; Ramar, Moothattu, & Balasubramanian, 2018; Zhang & Youngblood, 2023). The incorporation of nitrogen was notably higher in the samples synthesized with waste ethanol, regardless of temperature. This was evidenced by the peaks at 1475 cm−1 and 1613 cm−1 in both E-CDs-175 °C and E-CDs-200 °C. The enhanced nitrogen incorporation was attributed to the dissolution of nitrogen-containing proteins and peptides into ethanol during SF precipitation, where ethanol effectively utilized nitrogenous compounds in the CDs synthesis process.

XPS measurements (Fig. 4 ) was performed to characterize the chemical structure and elemental composition of the CDs. Peaks at 285.0 eV, 399.5 eV, and 532.3 eV were attributed to C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s, respectively. High-resolution spectra of C 1s were deconvoluted into four peaks: 284.9 eV (C–C/C=C), 286.3 eV (C–O), 288.2 eV (C=O), and 288.8 eV (sp2-bonded N-C=N). While all three samples contained a peak at 284.9 eV (C–C/C=C), the E-CDs lacked a peak at 286.3 eV (C–O). Instead, E-CDs showed a stronger signal for the well-oxidized group at 288.2 eV (C=O) compared to W-CDs. The N 1s spectra revealed peaks corresponding to N–H (401.5 eV) and N-C=N (398.5 eV) (Chowdhury & Kumar, 2021; Yin et al., 2023). Both E-CDs-175 °C and E-CDs-200 °C exhibited stronger N-C=N signals than W-CDs-200 °C, supporting the findings from the C 1s spectra. This suggested that the use of waste ethanol resulted in CDs with more complex structures. The O 1s spectra at 533.4 eV were attributed to -OH groups, with W-CDs showing a higher concentration of -OH groups compared to E-CDs. Overall, E-CDs-175 °C and E-CDs-200 °C exhibited similar XPS patterns, characterized by a higher presence of C=O and N-C=N groups and fewer -OH groups compared to W-CDs-200 °C.

The UV–vis absorption spectrum (Fig. 5 a) revealed that all samples exhibited an absorbance band at approximately λ ≈ 225 nm, characteristic of the π-π∗ transition of aromatic C=C bonds, indicating that sp2-hybridized carbon was predominant in the CDs structure (Chen et al., 2019). Both E-CDs-175 °C and E-CDs-200 °C showed enhanced absorbance at 275 nm, which was attributed to n-π∗ transitions of C=O bonds, corroborating the XPS results. Previous studies have confirmed that the use of organic solvents helped to minimize the breakage of carbon-oxygen, carbon-carbon, and carbon-nitrogen unsaturated bonds during hydrothermal treatment (Li et al., 2022; Onat, Izgi, Şahin, & Saka, 2024; Ozay, Tercan, & Ozay, 2024). The fluorescence spectra (Fig. 5b–g) demonstrated that all samples exhibited excitation-dependent photoluminescence, where the emission peak gradually red-shifted as the excitation wavelength They exhibited a consistent excitation peak at 360 nm and emission peak at 440 nm, demonstrating similar fluorescence properties across different synthesis conditions.

Considering the morphological characteristics, particle size, XRD, FTIR, XPS, UV–vis absorption, and fluorescence spectra, E-CDs-175 °C exhibited comparable physicochemical properties to E-CDs-200 °C. This suggested that 175 °C was sufficient to synthesize CDs from SF residue in ethanol media. Therefore, E-CDs-175 °C was selected for incorporation into gelatin/SFP films in the subsequent experiments. Additionally, since W-CDs-200 °C exhibited distinct properties compared to E-CDs-175 °C, it was also used to dope the gelatin/SFP films in order to evaluate how the properties of the CDs influenced the functional characteristics of the composite films.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Gelatin/SFP/CDs composite films formation and characterization

ce:section.ce:para: Rheology tests mainly aim to evaluate the viscosity and elasticity tendency of the hydrogel composite solution before film formation. This information is essential for understanding whether the material can be easily cast, spread, or sprayed onto the fruit surface. Fig. 6 a presented the viscosity of composite films as a function of shear rate. All film solutions exhibited shear-thinning behavior, owing to the disruption of entanglement between molecules under high-speed shear (Rizzo et al., 2018). The apparent viscosity of the films increased with the incorporation of CDs, and the resulting higher viscosity was primarily due to attractive force between nanoparticle and biomacromolecules. Non-covalent interactive bonds such as hydrophobic forces, hydrogen bonds and electrostatic interactions endowed by CD could accelerated the aggregation of gelatin. Higher CD content further strengthened these molecular forces, promoting the formation of a more cohesive, interlinked network and consequently improving the film-forming ability. These observations were consistent with the enhanced hydrogen bonding detected in the FTIR analysis, which contributed to the development of a more compact film system. This increased bonding played a key role in enhancing both the film morphology and the rheological properties.

The flow curves of the film solutions (Fig. 6b) were described by the Herschel-Bulkley model, with the corresponding parameters illustrated in Table S1. All flow curves fitted the Herschel-Bulkley model well, with high correlation coefficients (R2 > 0.99). The parameters of Herschel-Bulkley model further confirmed that the relationship between shear stress-shear rate of film solution system displayed shear-thinning behavior as a non-Newtonian fluid. The consistency index (κ) of the ternary films was significantly higher than those of single and binary films, signaling that CDs contributed to stabilizing the film matrix against stress. The improvement owed to the strong network structure formed by intermolecular interactions and increased viscosity of polymer system, in line with findings of Martínez-González et al. (2020). Compared to W-CDs-doped films, the higher κ in E-CDs-doped films indicated better mechanical stability within the gel matrix. Therefore, E-CDs facilitated the transition from flow to solid more effectively, requiring a stronger shear rate to disrupt the film matrix.

To further assess the dynamic rheology of the films within the linear viscoelastic range, the tan δ (loss-to-storage modulus ratio) was measured across varying frequencies. As shown in Fig. 6c, the viscoelastic behavior of the films demonstrated an increase in tan δ with rising frequency, with all samples exhibiting a modulus ratio below 1, indicating a tendency toward elasticity. In the presence of CDs, the gel exhibited greater robustness, as evidenced by lower tan δ values, particularly at higher frequencies. This enhancement in robustness was likely due to the CDs acting as nanofillers, which strongly cross-linked the polymer chains, thereby increasing resistance to film disruption (Tian et al., 2010). Overall, CDs improved the film properties in two key ways: by contributing to the polymerization of solution components into a cohesive film matrix, enhancing the film-forming ability, and by promoting network reorganization, stabilizing, and strengthening existing interactions. These effects were more pronounced at higher CD concentrations.

Chemical interactions among the constituents of the composite films were investigated via FTIR analysis (Fig. 7 a). The pure gelatin film displayed three prominent peaks at 1650 cm−1, 1542 cm−1, and 1230 cm−1, corresponding to the C=O stretching vibration of amide I, the N–H bending and C–N stretching vibration of amide II, and the N–H angular vibration and C–N stretching vibration of amide III, respectively. Additional absorption peaks around 3390 cm−1 and 2800 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibrations of N–H/O–H and C–H, respectively. Upon mixing with SFP, the characteristic amide peaks slightly shifted to lower wavenumbers, and the peak at 3282 cm−1 broadened. The incorporation of CDs into the composite films introduced new peaks at 1613 cm−1 and 1070 cm−1, corresponding to C=C/C=N and C–O–C functional groups, respectively. The presence of C-NH was indicated by the absorption peak at 1420 cm−1. The intensity of these newly formed peaks increased with higher CD loading, and a further blue shift of these characteristic peaks was observed. This suggested that these functional groups were tightly bonded to the neighboring structures via hydrogen bonding, contributing to the formation of a more compact and structurally stable film matrix.

The thermal stability of the composite films was assessed via TGA (Fig. 7b). The weight loss occurred in three distinct stages: the first (30–130 °C) corresponded to the evaporation of bound water, the second (180–280 °C) likely resulted from glycerol decomposition, and the major loss (200–400 °C) was attributed to the degradation of the gelatin matrix (Zhou et al., 2021). Pure gelatin and gelatin/SFP composites displayed similar decomposition profiles; however, the incorporation of CDs shifted the decomposition temperatures to higher values, enhancing network strength and improving thermal stability. Films with higher CD concentrations exhibited increased residual weight, indicating that CDs facilitated the formation of a more thermally stable char residue, acting as effective thermal insulators within the matrix. This behavior parallels that of carbon nanotubes (Dutta, Karak, Saikia, & Konwar, 2009). E-CDs demonstrated slightly superior thermal stability compared to W-CDs, as reflected in the higher residual weight, likely due to enhanced compatibility and stronger interactions with the gelatin/SFP matrix.

The surface morphology of the composite films is depicted in Fig. S2. The pure gelatin film exhibited a uniform and smooth microstructure, free of visible cracks. Upon incorporating SFP, the resulting gel surface became slightly rough with discernible cracks, in agreement with Li et al. (2023), indicating the formation of a crosslinked network structure between gelatin and SFP as the polymers were compressed into a confined space (Maroufi, Shahabi, Ghanbarzadeh, & Ghorbani, 2022). The introduction of CDs further enhanced the densification and roughness of the composite film surfaces. As the CD content increased, particularly at 1%, the surface displayed numerous granular structures due to CD agglomeration. Notably, films doped with E-CDs exhibited a more uniform morphology compared to those doped with W-CDs, suggesting that E-CDs were more evenly dispersed and demonstrated better compatibility within the gelatin/SFP matrix.

Fig. S3 illustrated the 2D topography, cross-sectional profile, 3D topography, and surface roughness of the composite films. The pure gelatin film was relatively flat, with a root mean square roughness (Rq) of 2.38 nm. The roughness of the gelatin/SFP film increased to 8.45 nm, due to the formation of tightly crosslinked structures among the polymers. The incorporation of 0.1% E-CDs reduced the Rq value to 6.36 nm, suggesting that this concentration facilitated the alignment of biopolymers into a more organized film, as previously reported (Soltanzadeh et al., 2022). This was further corroborated by the 2D and 3D surface topographies (Fig. S3). However, as the concentration of E-CDs increased, agglomeration within the gelatin/SFP matrix led to the formation of extruded aggregates and elevated surface roughness. In this scenario, CDs preferentially interacted with adjacent particles rather than with the polymers, causing partial phase separation. Previous studies have suggested that these aggregates enhance the material's UV-blocking capability by refracting UV radiation (Roy, Ezati, & Rhim, 2021). Consistent with SEM observations, E-CDs demonstrated greater miscibility and more homogeneous dispersion within the composite films compared to W-CDs. This enhanced compatibility resulted from the higher content of nitrogen- and oxygen-containing functional groups in E-CDs, which strengthened H-bonding interactions within the gelatin/SFP matrix (Eskalen, Çeşme, Kerli, & Özğan, 2021; Riahi, Priyadarshi, Rhim, & Bagheri, 2021).

Photolytic and photo-oxidative reactions, particularly those induced by UV light, accelerate food spoilage during storage. Consequently, packaging films with robust UV-shielding capabilities are essential to preserve food quality. Fig. 8 presented the transmittance spectra of various composite films in the 200–800 nm range. Pure gelatin and gelatin/SFP films exhibited poor UV-blocking performance, with relatively high UV transmittance at 280 nm (T280). The incorporation of CDs into the gelatin/SFP matrix significantly lowered the T280 value, thereby enhancing the UV-blocking ability. This improvement was primarily due to the UV absorption properties of CDs, as well as their large surface area, which facilitated light refraction and reduced transmittance. Notably, E-CDs provided more effective UV shielding, with the UV-blocking ability of gelatin/SFP/E-CDs-1.0% being 227% greater than that of gelatin/SFP/W-CDs-1.0%. This superior performance was attributed to the richer “n-π" interactions on the E-CDs surface, as evidenced by the XPS pattern, which absorbed UV irradiation and impeded light transmission (Ono et al., 2017). Li et al. (2023) confirmed loading of 3% pomelo peel-derived CDs could shield 20 % UV irradiation for gelatin films, and the blocking effect seemed to be much below than that of E-CDs, which was attributed to large number of n-π conjugated groups in the CDs surface gave it better UV absorption performance.

Food packaging materials are required to possess appropriate mechanical properties to ensure the effective protection of food during storage, transportation, and sales. As shown in Fig. 9 , the pure gelatin film exhibited a TS of 18.02 MPa and an EB of 46.87%. The addition of SFP had minimal impact on these mechanical properties, in line with the findings of Bu et al. (2023) for similar gelatin films. However, introducing CDs resulted in a sharp increase in TS, EB, and toughness values, denoting strongly improved mechanical performance. As zero-dimensional nanomaterials, CDs possessed internal disorder and a high surface area, which increased the film matrix density and promoted polymer chain cross-linking (Roy et al., 2021). Consequently, the CD-reinforced microstructure improved the network strength and overall durability of the films. Consistent with the findings of Li et al. (2023) and Roy et al. (2021), CDs significantly influenced the mechanical properties of gelatin-based films by forming strong hydrogen bonds with the biopolymer matrix. The gelatin/SFP/E-CDs films exhibited optimal mechanical properties at a 1.0% E-CDs concentration, achieving a TS of 32 MPa and EB of approximately 100%. These values much higher than previously prepared CD-doped gelatin film (Guo et al., 2024). The better biocompatibility and interfacial properties of SFP-derived CDs contributes to the firm H-bonded structures between the nanoparticles and polymers, which was responsible for more undesirable mechanical strength. In combination with their thermal properties, CDs not only served as nano-reinforcements but also enhanced the thermal stability of the packaged film systems.

The WCA of the composite films was depicted in Fig. 10 . Pure gelatin and gelatin/SFP films exhibited low WCAs of approximately 74.0° and 76.0°, respectively, reflecting relatively hydrophilic surfaces. Incorporating CDs obviously increased the WCA of the composite films, with higher CD concentrations leading to further elevation in hydrophobicity. This trend aligned with previous studies showing that the addition of tea-derived CDs enhanced the hydrophobicity of gelatin-based films (Konwar, Gogoi, Majumdar, & Chowdhury, 2015). Although both W-CDs and E-CDs increased hydrophobicity compared to gelatin/SFP films, W-CDs exhibited lower WCAs than E-CDs at the same concentrations.

The observed differences in WCA could be attributed to the combined effects of surface roughness and chemical composition. AFM results (Section 3.2.3) indicated that W-CDs-containing films exhibited higher surface roughness than E-CDs films. According to the Wenzel model, rougher surfaces amplified the wetting properties, making hydrophilic surfaces more hydrophilic (Wolansky & Marmur, 1999). This helped explain why W-CDs films, despite their increased roughness, exhibited lower WCAs due to their inherent hydrophilic characteristics. XPS results (Section 3.1.1) further supported this observation, showing that W-CDs contained more hydroxyl groups than E-CDs, making W-CDs films less hydrophobic. In contrast, E-CDs, with fewer hydroxyl groups, resulted in higher WCAs, even with smoother surfaces. Consequently, the variations in WCA were a result of the interplay between surface roughness and the presence of hydrophilic functional groups in the CDs.

Excessive oxygen is a key contributor to food spoilage and deterioration, making oxygen barrier properties a critical indicator for evaluating active packaging materials. As shown in Fig. 11 a, the pure gelatin film exhibited an OTR of 3.85 × 10−3 g/m2 s Pa, with no significant reduction upon blending with SFP. However, the OTR decreased to below 3 × 10−3 g/m2 s Pa with the inclusion of just 0.1% CDs. Increasing the CD concentration to 1% created additional hydrophilic space and more hydrogen-bonded sites (Zhang et al., 2016), further lowering the OTR values. Notably, films loaded with E-CDs demonstrated superior oxygen barrier performance compared to W-CDs, with a more stable and dense structure being key to the enhanced oxygen-blocking effect. Oladzadabbasabadi et al. (2023) reported that the unique structural characteristics of CDs enable tailored packaging materials, providing excellent resistance to oxygen transmission.

The WVP of the films was presented in Fig. 11b. The gelatin/SFP film exhibited a higher WVP than the pure gelatin film due to the hydrophilic -OH groups in SFP, which increased water affinity. Although CDs also possess hydrophilic properties (Purkayastha et al., 2014), CDs-doped films exhibited lower WVP values compared to gelatin/SFP. The reasons for this improvement include: (1) CD dispersion in the gelatin/SFP matrix reduced free volume and enhanced film compactness, partially blocking water vapor channels within the network (Purkayastha et al., 2014); (2) Increased network density led to enhanced surface roughness and cohesion, improving surface hydrophobicity (evident from the water contact angle); (3) Hydrogen bonding between CDs and polymers consumed free hydrophilic groups, further reducing film hydrophilicity. These combined effects counterbalanced the inherent hydrophilicity of the CDs. The strong reinforcing ability of CDs imparted exceptional barrier properties, offering significant potential to mitigate moisture- and oxygen-induced spoilage, ultimately extending the shelf life of packaged foods.

CDs have been shown to mitigate oxidation-induced food degradation by modulating free radical activity (Li et al., 2023; Rizzo et al., 2018). The antioxidant activities of the films, assessed via DPPH and ABTS radical scavenging assays, were illustrated in Fig. 11c and d. Both pure gelatin and gelatin/SFP films exhibited minimal radical scavenging capabilities (approximately 20%), indicating low antioxidant activity. The incorporation of 0.1% E-CDs or W-CDs enhanced the scavenging rates to 56.31% and 40.44%, and 50.79% and 39.38%, respectively. This increase was attributed to the presence of electron-donating groups, such as hydroxyl and carboxyl groups, on the surface of CDs, which facilitated the transfer of H• to ABTS• and DPPH•, thereby neutralizing the radicals (Roy et al., 2021). As the concentration of CDs increased, the antioxidant activities of the films were further enhanced. Films doped with E-CDs exhibited superior antioxidant performance compared to those doped with W-CDs, likely due to the higher density of carbonyl groups on the surface of E-CDs.

Fig. 12 demonstrated the antibacterial effects of various gelatin-based films against S. aureus (a gram-positive bacterium) and E. coli (a gram-negative bacterium). The colony-forming units (CFU) of both bacteria were significantly inhibited by CD-doped films (both E-CDs and W-CDs), especially following UV irradiation. Compared to the control group, where S. aureus and E. coli began to proliferate after incubation, the films incorporating 1.0% CDs exhibited a marked enhancement in antimicrobial efficacy, as evidenced by a significant reduction in bacterial CFU, highlighting the superior antibacterial activity of these composite films. The antibacterial rates of the gelatin-based films against S. aureus and E. coli were illustrated in Fig. 13 . Incorporating 1.0% CDs significantly boosted the antimicrobial efficacy of the composite films, with inhibition rates for S. aureus and E. coli exceeding 85% and 65%, respectively. These findings aligned with those of Das et al. (2022), who reported that clove-derived CDs exhibited high lethality against S. aureus. Zhang and Youngblood (2023) similarly observed significant wrinkling and rupture of bacterial cell membranes following the incorporation of guanidinium-based CDs. Additionally, the positively charged groups on CDs facilitated electrostatic interactions with bacterial surfaces, disrupting the osmotic barrier and accelerating cell disintegration and leakage of intracellular contents. Furthermore, the antibacterial effect was more pronounced against S. aureus than E. coli, likely due to structural differences between the bacteria. Gram-positive bacteria are more susceptible to harmful compounds due to the lack of an outer membrane, while long-chain lipopolysaccharides in gram-negative bacteria prevent the entry of CD-based substances.

Extra UV irradiation further enhanced the antibacterial activity, particularly against E. coli. The E-CDs-doped film exhibited a moderate antibacterial rate of 70% without UV irradiation (Fig. 13b), which increased dramatically to 95% upon UV exposure. This suggests that CDs act as photosensitizers, where UV-activated C=N and C=O groups generate ROS via a photodynamic mechanism, contributing to bacterial eradication (Chen et al., 2023b). These results demonstrated that the incorporation of CDs, particularly E-CDs with abundant C=N and C=O groups, significantly enhanced the antibacterial properties of composite films, underscoring the potential of CD-based materials for the development of active packaging films.

Blueberries are valued for their nutritional content and health benefits, rich in protein, anthocyanins, dietary fiber, and various vitamins and minerals (Xu, Chen, Zhang, & Xu, 2023). However, they are highly prone to decay after harvest, often spoiling within 1–2 days under ambient storage conditions (Li et al., 2024c; Sheng, Tsai, Zhu, & Zhu, 2019). As discussed in Section 3.2, gelatin/SFP/E-CDs-1.0% and gelatin/SFP/W-CDs-1.0% composite films, exhibiting satisfactory functional properties, were employed as coatings for blueberry preservation in this study.

Fig. 14 a illustrated the increase in decay incidence of blueberries over the course of storage. The control group (CK) showed the highest decay incidence due to microbial infection, whereas the CD-doped composite films effectively inhibited blueberry decay throughout the 8-day storage period. The evaluation period aligned with the study by Zhang and Youngblood (2023), where blueberries were stored under similar conditions at 25 °C and 50% RH. In the study by Ren et al. (2022), a longer storage period of 15 days achieved at a lower storage temperature of 4 °C. Regarding darkening incidence (Fig. 14b), blueberries began to darken after 2 days of storage. In contrast to the CK group, pure gelatin, and gelatin/SFP films, those coated with CD-doped films significantly slowed the darkening process. Over time, the commercially acceptable rate of blueberries decreased (Fig. 14c). Compared to the CK and gelatin/SFP groups, blueberries coated with CD-doped films maintained a higher commercially acceptable rate, with approximately 80% of the fruit remaining consumable after 8 days, versus only 40% for the CK group, necessitating the termination of the evaluation. This improvement might be attributed to the enhanced antioxidant activity and microbial resistance imparted by the CDs, which helped protect the fruit from spoilage. These findings aligned with Zhao et al. (2022), who reported that incorporating CDs into polymer films prolonged the shelf life of jujubes.

Firmness is a crucial parameter for evaluating post-harvest fruit quality (Chang, Liang, Guo, Wang, & Yang, 2023; Shi et al., 2022). Fig. 14d illustrated that the firmness of blueberries decreased during storage. Compared to the CK group, blueberries preserved by gelatin-based films maintained superior firmness, especially those coated with E-CDs-doped films. This improvement is attributed to the lower oxygen permeability value of CD-doped films, which decreased fruit respiration intensity and delayed the softening process. It was consistent with the weight loss rate shown in Fig. 14e, where respiration-induced water and carbohydrate loss affected the taste and appearance of the fruit. Blueberries preserved by gelatin/SFP film exhibited a significantly lower weight loss rate than the CK group (13.5% after 8 days). Incorporating CDs further reduced the weight loss rate to less than 5%. This reduction can be attributed to: (1) a low-oxygen environment that limits the activity of malate dehydrogenase enzyme, thereby delaying carbohydrate consumption (Barkaoui et al., 2021); and (2) the enhanced mechanical properties and barrier effects of the films, which restrict weight loss (Martínez-González et al., 2020). The content of soluble solids, which reflects the total amount of soluble sugars, amino acids, and pectin, serves as an important indicator of fruit nutritional value (Araújo et al., 2024). Fig. 14f showed that the soluble solids content of blueberries initially increased during the first 2 days due to the conversion of insoluble starch to soluble sugars, then gradually decreased over the storage period as these nutrients were consumed by respiration and spoilage organisms. Packaged blueberries with gelatin-based films mitigated this decline caused by their respiration inhibition and antimicrobial properties. Notably, after 8 days, the soluble solids content of blueberries preserved with CD-doped films remained comparable to the original levels, indicating effective preservation of nutritional value.

Fig. S4a showed that the MDA content in the CK group increased from 2.14 mg/g to 5.26 mg/g over the storage period. However, the increase in MDA for blueberries preserved with gelatin-based films was much slower, particularly in those with CD-doped films, indicating that CDs effectively mitigate oxidative injury. Anthocyanins, responsible for the blue and purple hues of blueberries, are primarily found in the fruit's peel, which is sensitive to water, oxygen, light, and high temperature (Huang et al., 2023). During storage, TAC initially increased, followed by a decrease (Fig. S4b). The initial increase was due to anthocyanin biosynthesis during ripening, while the subsequent decline occurred when oxidation outpaced biosynthesis. Blueberries packaged with CD-doped films exhibited a delayed ripening process, extending the anthocyanin accumulation period to 4 days compared to 2 days for other treatments. The decrease in TAC was limited in blueberries packaged with CD-doped films, owing to the oxygen-, water-, and UV light-blocking abilities of the films, which suppressed anthocyanin oxidation, and helped maintain fruit quality after harvest.

According to the results, all the films utilized in this study—including gelatin, gelatin/SFP, and CDs-doped gelatin/SFP films—demonstrated their effectiveness in extending the shelf life and preserving the quality of blueberries during storage at 25 °C. Notably, the gelatin/SFP/E-CDs-1.0% film exhibited superior preservation performance. Other studies also investigated the preservative effects of composite films on postharvest blueberries. In the study of Liu and Xin (2023), the blueberries were packaged by the gelatin/chitosan films incorporated with bioactive materials (monascus red, phycocyanin, and safflower yellow), and were stored at 25 °C. After 6 days, the functional films delayed the weight loss of fruit and the decrease in hardness loss rates. Wang et al. (2024) developed a gelatin film with 30% chitosan (CS) and ginger essential oil (GEO) nanoemulsions (G-NEs-30%), showing strong preservative effects on blueberries stored at (20 ± 2) °C for 9 days. The G-NEs-30% film reduced decay (30% vs. 75% by day 6), minimized weight loss (12% vs. 25% by day 9), and maintained higher firmness compared to the control. The study of Hossen et al. (2024) and Piechowiak, Grzelak-Błaszczyk, Sójka, Skóra, and Balawejder (2022) demonstrated the preservative effects of composite coatings on blueberries. Hossen utilized a chitosan/gelatin coating with a ginger essential oil/β-cyclodextrin complex, preserving blueberries for 16 days at 25 °C and 40 days at 4 °C, while Piechowiak used a starch-gelatin film with cinnamon oil, maintaining quality and antioxidant activity for 10 days at 4 °C. In our study, we fully utilize Sargassum fusiforme polysaccharides (SFP), residual S. fusiforme waste from polysaccharide extraction, and ethanol discarded during purification, built on our previous work. This research underscores the potential of circular economy practices by upcycling marine seaweed residues and waste of the food industry into high-performance, eco-friendly packaging for food preservation.

Blueberries often end up in landfills at the end of their lifecycle, contributing to GHG emissions and exacerbating global warming potential (Zhang, Tong, Singh, Cai, & Huang, 2019). The use of composite films for blueberry preservation can significantly reduce decay rates, mitigating food waste and the associated GHG emissions. However, much of the existing research has concentrated on developing composite films for fruit preservation without quantifying the resultant reduction in GHG emissions due to the extended shelf life of the preserved fruit. Fig. 15 illustrated the GHG emissions associated with preserving 1 kg of blueberries using different films. The control group exhibited a GHG emission of 0.48 kg CO2-equivalent (CO2-eq), while the use of gelatin/SFP film for blueberry preservation resulted in a significantly lower emission of 0.28 kg CO2-eq. Further incorporation of CDs reduced GHG emissions to 0.16 and 0.17 kg CO2-eq for E-CDs and W-CDs, respectively, consistent with the findings of Zhang et al. (2019). In 2021, global blueberry production amounted to 1,113,260 tons (Hellström, Karhu, Karhu, Järvenpää, & Välimaa, 2024). If all blueberries were preserved using E-CDs-doped films, an estimated reduction of approximately 3.56 × 108 kg CO2-eq emissions could be achieved compared to the control group.

Additionally, the potential environmental impacts of synthesizing E-CDs at 175 °C and W-CDs at 200 °C were compared through LCA, based on a functional unit of 1 kg of CDs. The GHG emissions for synthesizing E-CDs at 175 °C were found to be 11,168 kg CO2-eq, which is lower than the 15,085 kg CO2-eq emissions from synthesizing W-CDs at 200 °C. This suggests that using waste ethanol at a relatively lower temperature for CDs synthesis is more environmentally favorable than using water at a higher temperature. Torsello et al. (2024) reported a GHG emission of 19,200 kg CO2-eq when using citric acid as a precursor for CDs synthesis, which is higher than our results. This discrepancy can be attributed to their use of citric acid as a precursor, whereas our study utilized waste SF residue. It was noteworthy that limited efforts had been made to evaluate the potential environmental impacts of different CDs synthesis routes. Therefore, further research on this topic is highly recommended.

This study presents a novel and sustainable strategy for designing high-performance fruit packaging films by incorporating CDs synthesized from marine SFP extraction residues. The multifunctional films developed offer a significant step forward in addressing food packaging challenges, demonstrating exceptional mechanical strength, UV resistance, thermal stability, and hydrophobicity, with optimal performance achieved at a 1.0% E-CDs concentration. The CDs-doped films exhibited robust antioxidant activity and strong antibacterial efficacy against S. aureus and E. coli. Practical applications of the composite films demonstrated their effectiveness in extending the shelf life of blueberries by reducing decay, maintaining firmness, and preserving nutritional quality. These results highlight the potential of the films to maintain food quality during storage. Moreover, the synthesis of CDs using waste ethanol, as opposed to distilled water, resulted in a lower global warming potential, underscoring the environmental benefits and circular economy potential of this approach. However, the detailed antimicrobial mechanism was not completely explained, and the generality of the antimicrobial ability on pathogenic bacteria and preservation effect on different fruits will be investigated in further study. Overall, this research not only offers an innovative solution for eco-friendly food packaging but also exemplifies the potential of upcycling marine seaweed residues into high-value materials.
